title: Use consistent elevation levels
summary: AI-generated interfaces often apply shadows at different intensities without a clear elevation system, making depth feel arbitrary rather than hierarchical.
instruction: Define 2-4 elevation levels and assign them to specific component roles. Do not vary shadow size, opacity, or blur beyond these defined levels. Most surfaces should be flat (level 0).
rationale: Shadows communicate what users can interact with and what's above the surface. When every element has a different shadow, depth loses meaning and the interface feels visually chaotic. Consistent elevation builds a mental model of the interface's spatial structure.
violations: A card has a 10px blur shadow, a floating button uses 8px, a modal uses 24px, a tooltip uses 6px, and a dropdown uses 12px - five different elevations with no system.
An alert banner has a heavier shadow than the modal it overlays, inverting the expected depth hierarchy.
controls: A design with three elevations: level 0 (flat - all cards, panels, surfaces), level 1 (4px blur - interactive elements on hover), level 2 (12px blur - modals and drawers).
A marketing page using zero shadows on all surfaces, with depth communicated purely through color and size contrast.
borderlineCases: A component library may need 4-5 elevations for different interactive states (rest, hover, active, focus, drag). The key is that these map to state, not random values.
contextualExceptions: Custom OS-level chrome (title bar overlays, system dialogs).
3D or spatial interfaces where depth is literal.
detectorSignal: CSS box-shadow declarations with more than 3 distinct blur-radius values across the codebase
matcherProposal: Parse all box-shadow declarations. Extract blur radius (second value in standard syntax). Group by blur value. Flag if more than 4 distinct blur groups exist. Exclude inset shadows and multiple-shadow declarations that create compound effects.
detectorLimitations: Multiple comma-separated shadows on one element may create a single elevation effect from multiple blur values.
Tailwind-style utility shadows (shadow-sm, shadow-md, shadow-lg) have defined blur values that should be counted as their semantic level.
Filter: drop-shadow() is a different CSS mechanism.
falsePositiveScenarios: A button design that uses multiple shadows for a single effect (e.g., ambient + directional shadow = one elevation level).
A design system with 5 defined elevation tokens may still only use 2-3 in practice.
mitigationStrategy: Count distinct elevation levels (semantic groupings of blur values within 15% tolerance), not raw blur values. Flag only when codebase has 5+ distinct groups.

fixturePlans.violation: A page using shadows with blur values: 4px, 8px, 10px, 16px, 20px, 24px across different components.
fixturePlans.control: A page using shadows only as: 0px (flat surfaces), 6px (elevated cards), 16px (modals).
fixturePlans.borderline: A page using shadow-sm, shadow, shadow-md, shadow-lg, shadow-xl from Tailwind where some are composition shadows - count semantic levels not raw values.

cleanRoomAttestations: Original observation. Elevation systems documented in Material Design and HIG.
overlapNotes: Related to depth.shadow-soup (which flags combining multiple depth techniques). This is about inconsistent elevation values within the same depth language. Complementary - shadow-soup flags mixing techniques, this flags inconsistent application of one technique.
